votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the intersperse logic using iteration and slicing, producing the same results as the Coq recursive definition for empty, single-element, and multi-element lists.
The Python code correctly implements the intersperse logic, handling empty lists, single-element lists, and multi-element lists exactly as the Coq specification does.
The Python implementation correctly inserts the delimiter between elements of the list, matching the behavior of the Coq specification for all concrete inputs.

The Python code correctly implements the same logic as the Coq code. The Coq intersperse_spec_aux recursively builds a list by inserting the delimiter between consecutive elements. The Python version does the same by iterating through all elements except the last, appending each element followed by the delimiter, then appending the last element. Both handle empty lists (returning []) and single-element lists (returning [x]) correctly. The spec function in both cases checks if result equals the computed interspersed list.

The Python code correctly implements the same logic as the Coq code. The Coq intersperse_spec_aux recursively builds a list by inserting the delimiter between consecutive elements, returning [] for empty list and [x] for singleton. The Python version does the same: for empty list returns [], otherwise appends each element except the last followed by delimiter, then appends the last element. Both produce identical results for all concrete inputs.

The Python code correctly implements the same logic as the Coq code. The Coq recursive function intersperse_spec_aux inserts the delimiter between consecutive elements, and the Python version does the same by appending each element except the last followed by the delimiter, then appending the last element. Both handle empty lists (returning []) and single-element lists (returning [x]) correctly. The spec function in both cases checks equality of result with the computed interspersed list.
Both produce the list with the delimiter inserted between elements (none at end), handling empty and singleton lists identically; the spec is a direct equality check.

Both implementations produce a list with the delimiter interleaved between elements, handling empty and singleton lists identically; the Python boolean spec matches the Coq equality-based spec on all concrete inputs.
Both produce [] for empty, keep singletons unchanged, and otherwise insert the delimiter between each pair of elements; the Python spec function checks equality exactly as the Coq Prop specifies.
